target_emits.name: create_policy_shared
target_emits.purpose: Create the policy profile in the Library. The big aos8_acl_sess_to_central_policy_rules transform builds the policy-rule[] array from the AOS 8 acl_sess record (both v4 + v6 rules) plus role_attribution from runtime_values.
target_emits.iteration_notes: One POST per ACL. The whole policy-rule[] array is computed by the 2-arg transform via ctx (source_data + runtime_values).

target_emits.name: assign_policy
target_emits.purpose: Assign the policy profile to the target scope. Multi-device-function array packed into a single POST.

config-assignment: {one entry per device_function in target_meta.device_functions: { scope-id, device-function, profile-type='policy', profile-instance='{name}' }}

target_emits.iteration_notes: One POST; all device-functions share the array.

target_meta.device_function_filtering_rule: Per the operator's 'x-supportedDeviceType is field-honored, not body-gated' clarification: a single policy body works across all device-functions. Each device honors only the fields applicable to it (e.g. RULE_TCP / transport-fields are honored on Switch CX / AP / Switch PVOS; RULE_PROTOCOL with ip-header.protocol is live-verified to be honored on Gateway despite schema marking it AP-only). For AOS 8 -> Central GW migration we target MOBILITY_GW; the body shape is permissive and other device-functions could be added if AP / Switch use cases land later.

target_scope_id_resolution.rule: Central scope_id is supplied by the consuming skill at runtime, derived from the AOS 8 acl_sess record's binding scope.
target_scope_id_resolution.input: Source binding scope (string path / identifier); skill-specific

dependencies.depends_on: central:role (the roles in role_attribution must already exist in Central before policies referencing them are POSTed)

depended_by.relation: Central back-fills the role's policies[] array from policy.policy-rule[].condition.source.role-list automatically — this translation INVERTS the AOS 8 role->ACL binding direction.
depended_by.notes: AOS 8 has role.role__acl[] referencing ACL names; Central has policy.policy-rule[].condition.source.role-list referencing role names. Migration creates policies that reference roles, and Central derives the role->policies back-reference.

objects.live_shape_example: [{"accname": "missing-policies", "acl_sess__v4policy": [{"sany": true, "src": "sany", "dany": true, "dst": "dany", "echo": true, "icmp_type": "echo", "svc": "icmp", "service_app": "service", "permit": true, "action": "permit"}, {"suser": true, "src": "suser", "dipaddr": "10.1.1.1", "dst": "dhost", "service-name": "svc-http", "svc": "service-name", "service_app": "service", "dnatport": 8080, "action": "dst-nat"}, {"suser": true, "src": "suser", "dany": true, "dst": "dany", "tungrpname": "test", "re_dir": "tunnel-group", "action": "redir_opt"}], "acl_sess__v6policy": []}]
objects.notes: Live-verified across 88 user-customized ACLs in the operator's tenant + a purpose-configured 'missing-policies' ACL exercising the variants not present elsewhere (host destination, ICMP-echo, time-range reference, standalone dst-nat, redirect-tunnel + redirect-tunnel-group, destination user-role, destination local-IP, app-deny). Each ACL has parallel acl_sess__v4policy and acl_sess__v6policy rule arrays; the translation's transform merges both into a single Central policy-rule[] array tagged with address-family. Inheritance: filter _flags.inherited / _flags.system entries before passing — the transform also defensively skips inherited/system rules so a stray inherited entry doesn't break the build.

Used ONLY for the role_attribution lookup — for each ACL being translated, scan all role records and collect rname values where role__acl[] contains an entry with acl_type='session' and pname matching the ACL's accname. The collected list is passed to emit_calls via runtime_values['role_attribution']. The translation does NOT walk role records itself; the consumer pre-computes role_attribution before calling emit_calls. Filter _flags.system / _flags.default / _flags.readonly ACL entries on the role side too (system role-ACL bindings shouldn't migrate).

merge_rule.description: For each acl_sess record, build role_attribution by reverse-indexing across all role records: collect rname values where role.role__acl[].pname == acl.accname AND role.role__acl[].acl_type == 'session'. Filter system / default / readonly entries on the role side. The result is the list of roles for which Central will need source.role-list to point when an AOS 8 rule has src='suser' or dst='duser' (the implicit 'user-of-this-role' semantic). Passed to emit_calls via runtime_values['role_attribution']; consumer responsibility.
merge_rule.inheritance_handling: Drop role records with top-level _flags.default=true (system roles like 'logon', 'guest', 'voice', etc. — Central has its own copies). Drop individual role__acl[] entries with _flags.system / _flags.readonly / _flags.default.

name.to: policy name (Central path param + body 'name', profile-instance on step 2)

policy_rules.notes: 2-arg transform: the engine inspects the signature and passes ctx (source_data + runtime_values) as the second arg. The transform ignores the 'from' value (using accname only as a stable lookup target) and reads acl_sess__v4policy + acl_sess__v6policy directly from ctx['source_data'], plus role_attribution from ctx['runtime_values']. Returns the full Central policy-rule[] array; engine substitutes it into the body wherever {policy_rules} appears. Empty / no-rules ACLs return None (per LLD 'empty ACL not migrated' rule); engine drops the body key, leaving security-policy.policy-rule absent — operator should filter ACLs with empty rule lists from migration before calling emit_calls.

unmapped_fields.from: AOS 8 rules with apptosstr / appprio8021p / queue / queue_type (QoS marking)
unmapped_fields.reason: Central's QoS / packet-marking action sub-shapes (action.packet-marking, action.policing, action.qos) exist but the AOS 8 source-side mapping is not yet authored. AOS 8 carries TOS strings, 802.1p priorities, and queue selectors on rules; Central uses DSCP / dot1p enums via separate action sub-objects. Deferred to a v2 of this translation; the transform currently drops these fields silently.
unmapped_fields.todo: Configure AOS 8 ACLs with QoS marking + capture; add packet-marking / policing builders to _build_action.

unmapped_fields.from: AOS 8 rules with src-nat / route actions
unmapped_fields.reason: src-nat (ACTION_SOURCE_NAT) and route (ACTION_ROUTE / PBR) actions are mapped to Central enum values but the action sub-config (source-nat.{ip,pool}, routing-action.{tunnel-group, dscp}) is not yet built by the transform. Less common than dst-nat/dual-nat/redirect; deferred to v2.

unmapped_fields.from: AOS 8 rules with mirror / blacklist / captive action options
unmapped_fields.reason: Less common action variants without verified Central body shapes; deferred to v2 once we have configured live samples.

unmapped_fields.from: AOS 8 ADDRESS_GROUP / ADDRESS_DOMAIN_NAME / ADDRESS_AP_* / ADDRESS_MASTER_IP / ADDRESS_USER source/destination types
unmapped_fields.reason: Central enum has 14 source/destination address types; the translation maps the 7 most common (ANY, HOST, NETWORK, ALIAS, ROLE, LOCAL, USER). The remaining 7 (DOMAIN_NAME, GROUP, AP_IP, AP_IP_ALL, AP_NETWORK, MASTER_IP, SUBNET_MASK) have no direct AOS 8 source-side discriminator we've verified. Add per discriminator as configured samples surface.

Operator scoped these out at translation authoring time (rarely used; better handled by alternative products). Translation handles redirect tunnel + redirect tunnel-group only. AOS 8 source field names for esi-group (re_dir='esi-group' + name + direction) and datapath (re_dir='datapath' + ID) are inferred from operator description; not live-verified.

AOS 8 CLI grammar treats source-spec / destination-spec as a single token (one of: user, user-role <name>, host <ip>, network <addr> <mask>, alias <name>, any) — no composition. Central's ArubaPolicyCondition_SourceRoleOptions / DestinationRoleOptions sub-schemas allow composing role-based matching with additional address constraints; this is a Central-native enhancement with no AOS 8 source path. Verified live: across 88 ACLs in the operator's tenant, zero rules combine a role discriminator with an additional address constraint on the same side.

ignored_variants.reason: AOS 8 system / built-in ACLs (global-sacl, apprf-*-sacl, etc.) appear with system flags. Migrating them creates duplicate policies in Central that shadow Central's own built-ins. Consumer must filter these from the role-attribution lookup.

ignored_variants.form: Empty acl_sess record (rule list empty after filtering)
ignored_variants.reason: Per the AOS 8 -> CNX LLD: 'Empty ACL will NOT be migrated.' The transform returns None for empty rule arrays; the engine drops policy-rule from the body. Consumer should typically filter empty ACLs from the migration set rather than POSTing a rule-less policy.

ignored_variants.form: ACL not attached to any role (role_attribution = empty list)
ignored_variants.reason: Per the LLD: 'ACL by itself will NOT be migrated.' The transform builds rules with ADDRESS_ANY for suser/duser when role_attribution is empty (rather than producing an invalid empty role-list). Consumer should typically skip ACLs with no role attribution rather than POSTing them with degraded source/destination semantics.

draft_notes: v1 translation, Gateway-targeted. POST /policies/{name} with security-policy.policy-rule[] computed by the aos8_acl_sess_to_central_policy_rules 2-arg transform.

INVERSION CALL-OUT (the central:role translation deferred role__acl to here): AOS 8 binds ACL->role via role.role__acl[] (role-side reference). Central inverts — policies reference roles via policy-rule[].condition.source.role-list (policy-side reference) and Central back-fills role.policies[] automatically. The translation receives role_attribution: list[str] (roles that reference this ACL) via runtime_values; consumer pre-computes by scanning role records.

Live-verified across 88 user-customized ACLs at /md/Campus/West + a purpose-configured 'missing-policies' ACL exercising 7 variant shapes (host destination, ICMP echo, time-range, dst-nat standalone, redirect-tunnel, redirect-tunnel-group, dst=duserrole, dst=dlocalip).

ANY-ANY EXPANSION (operator-confirmed): Central can express 'any -> user/host/network' or 'user -> any' for a role-bound policy, but NOT 'any -> any'. AOS 8 'any any' semantics are bidirectional (allow both directions). The translation expands one AOS 8 'any any' rule into TWO Central rules — 'role -> any' AND 'any -> role' — so return traffic is matched. Live-verified pattern in the operator's PD-allowall policy. Rules where AOS 8 source is any but destination is specific (any -> host, any -> network, any -> role) keep source: ADDRESS_ANY since Central does support those patterns; only the literal any-any pattern triggers expansion. When role_attribution is empty, no expansion happens — single-rule any-any is emitted (defensive; consumer should typically filter such ACLs per the LLD).

NAMED svc-* services use Central's authoritative net-service catalog. The translation passes AOS 8 service-name (svc-http, svc-https, svc-dns, svc-icmp, etc.) into Central's services.net-service field with rule-type RULE_NET_SERVICE. Central ships 73 net-services (verified via central_get_net_services) including 64 svc-* names mirroring AOS 8's convention. Unknown names produce a clean Central error instead of silent mistranslation to wrong protocol+port. Known structural mismatches (if any surface) are aliased via _AOS8_TO_CENTRAL_SVC_NAME_ALIASES in transforms.py.

Schema annotations on Central /policies are unreliable for Gateway support: many fields (rule-type, ip-header.protocol, transport-fields, source.type) are tagged 'Switch CX / AP / Switch PVOS' or 'AP only', yet the live tenant's GW-bound policies clearly USE them (RULE_PROTOCOL with ip-header.protocol=80 verified in amazon-device policy on a mobility gateway). Per operator clarification: 'fields tagged for specific devices apply only to those devices and are ignored by others' — the body itself is universal across device-types and the consumer's config-assignments POST is what binds it. Trust live data over schema device-type annotations.

Enum mappings (services.application / app-category / web-category / web-reputation) come from policy.json's x-enumDescriptions table — extracted at authoring time into translations/policy_enum_tables.py. 21/85 web-category entries insert connective words ('AND') between slash-separated AOS 8 forms (e.g. entertainment/arts -> ENTERTAINMENT-AND-ARTS) — a naive uppercase + slash-to-dash transform produces wrong values for those. Always use the explicit lookup table.

AOS 8 named-service -> Central protocol+port lookup: AOS 8's svc-* names (svc-icmp, svc-dns, svc-dhcp, svc-http, svc-https, etc.) have no Central CNX named-service enum equivalent. Each maps to a (protocol, optional port range) tuple via the _AOS8_NAMED_SERVICE_TO_CENTRAL table in transforms.py. Common services covered; uncommon svc-* names fall through to RULE_ANY semantics — consumer should surface a finding.

TCP/UDP+port shape inferred from schema, not live-verified: live-verification confirms RULE_PROTOCOL + ip-header.protocol works on GW (amazon-device policy). RULE_TCP / RULE_UDP with transport-fields.destination-port follow the schema's parallel structure — the translation produces this shape but it should be verified against the operator's tenant before treating as authoritative for production migration. RULE_PROTOCOL fallback is honored regardless.

Dual-stack (acl_sess__v4policy + acl_sess__v6policy) is collapsed into one Central policy-rule[] array with each rule's condition.address-family set to IPV4 or IPV6 accordingly. Position numbers are sequential across the merged array.
Idempotency: if a policy with the same name already exists, the SHARED POST returns HTTP 409. Consumer must handle 409 idempotently.
